_meta.source: build_companies.py — aggregates per-company JSON files in data/companies/
_meta.notes: Each company entry is hand-curated from that company's OWN published material (annual report, ESG/sustainability report, asset location pages). data_quality='sourced' means at least 5 country claims with strong evidence. Frontend should fall back to the modeled commodity-overlap heuristic for companies not present in this file.

country_claims.evidence: ADM operates the largest US soybean origination network, with 220+ grain elevators and crush plants in the corn belt. Disclosed in ADM 10-K segment reporting (Ag Services & Oilseeds segment, North America).
ADM operates 35+ Brazilian soybean origination assets and crush plants (Mato Grosso, Paraná, Goiás). Brazil disclosed as a major South America origination country in ADM 10-K and ESG report.
ADM operates ports + crush at Rosario and other Argentine facilities; Argentine soy disclosed alongside Brazilian in 10-K South America segment.
ADM operates a Paraguayan soy origination + crushing footprint (Villeta plant).
ADM is one of the top 3 US corn buyers; corn elevators concentrated in IL, IN, IA, NE, MN per public asset list.
ADM disclosed in 10-K as a major Brazilian corn origination party, especially in Mato Grosso / second-crop (safrinha) corn.
ADM owns/operated 4 Ukrainian assets (Donetsk, Odesa, Mykolaiv, Illichivsk). 2022-2025 operating status disrupted by war; ADM continues some Ukrainian grain origination.
ADM is a top-3 US wheat handler with elevators across the Great Plains.
ADM acquired Toepfer International and operates in Australian wheat sourcing.
Same Ukrainian asset disclosure as for maize; ADM is one of multiple international traders with continued Ukrainian wheat origination.

ADM has historically been one of the top 3 cocoa buyers from CIV; partial sale to Olam in 2015 reduced exposure but ADM retains chocolate / ingredient cocoa sourcing through its Cocoa & Wild Flavors business.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume percentages by country
Ukrainian asset operating status post-2022 not fully transparent in public filings
South America (Brazil + Argentina + Paraguay) reported as a region, not always split
ADM.meta_notes: ADM is publicly listed; the 10-K (Annual Report on Form 10-K, filed annually with the SEC) and the Corporate Sustainability Report disclose origination footprint at a regional, occasionally country, level. As with other traders, exact tonnage by country-of-origin is generally not disclosed.

Bunge.hq: Registered office: Geneva, Switzerland; operational HQ: Chesterfield, Missouri, USA

country_claims.evidence: Bunge is the first global commodity exporter to achieve 100% traceability and monitoring of its direct and indirect soy purchases in priority regions of the Brazilian Cerrado (Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, Bahia, Mato Grosso); direct soy monitored since 2020, indirect added in October 2024.

Bunge operates nine oilseed crush facilities and six grain mills in the US Midwest; key soy crush plants disclosed in 10-K including Council Bluffs IA, Decatur IN and Morristown IN, drawing from the major US soy belt (IL, IA, IN, MO, OH, NE, MN, WI, SD, ND).

Bunge operates oilseed processing capacity in Argentina and has equity-method investments in Argentine biodiesel producers; Argentina disclosed as a core South American soy origination country in the FY2024 10-K.
Bunge holds a 33% interest in Complejo Agroindustrial Angostura S.A. (CAIASA), an oilseed processing JV with Louis Dreyfus and Aceitera General Deheza located in Paraguay.

Bunge identifies corn (alongside wheat) as one of its principal sourced grains; historically operated US corn milling at Danville IL, Worthington IN, Crete NE, Red Oak IA, Muleshoe TX and Atchison KS, though this North America corn-milling business was agreed for sale to Grain Craft in April 2025.
Bunge's Brazilian agribusiness includes corn origination through its grain elevator and port network; corn is part of Bunge's principal sourced grains disclosed in the 10-K.
Bunge operated a corn milling plant in Queretaro, Mexico as part of its North America corn milling business (now under agreement to be sold to Grain Craft, April 2025).

Bunge operates seven wheat mills in Brazil including Moinho Fluminense in Rio de Janeiro (>600,000 t/yr capacity), one of the four largest mills in Latin America; brands include Suprema, Soberana, Farina, Predileta, and Bem-fácil.
Bunge lists wheat as one of its principal sourced grains and operates grain milling facilities in the US; wheat origination is part of the Agribusiness segment disclosed in the 10-K.

Bunge Ukraine operates an industrial transshipping complex in Mykolaiv (Nikolayev) including an oilseed crushing facility and upgraded grain terminal; Bunge sources wheat and grain through this Black Sea infrastructure.

Bunge operates a soybean and rapeseed crush and soybean oil refining facility at the Port of Amsterdam; the Amsterdam + Brest facilities together have ~2 million tons of annual processing capacity per Bunge's own disclosure.
Bunge operates a soybean and rapeseed crush facility at the Port of Brest, France, part of the two-site European complex with ~2 million tons combined annual capacity.
Bunge's Mykolaiv complex is described by Bunge as the largest export crushing facility in Ukraine and the leading sunflower oil facility in the world's largest sunflower seed origin.
Bunge operates conventional biodiesel facilities in Brazil that process soybean oil, and runs major soybean crush facilities supplying the Brazilian edible-oil and biofuel markets.
Bunge's nine US oilseed crush facilities produce soybean oil for food, animal feed and biofuel markets including renewable diesel feedstock supply.

Bunge held a 50% interest in BP Bunge Bioenergia, a JV with bp operating 11 sugarcane/ethanol mills across Brazil's Southeast, North and Midwest; Bunge announced in June 2024 the sale of its 50% share to bp (closing Q4 2024). After this divestiture Bunge no longer has a direct Brazilian sugar/ethanol footprint.

Bunge produces, blends and distributes NPK fertilizers including phosphate-based and nitrogen products through its Argentine operations; Bunge built a phosphate production facility at the Ramallo industrial complex and formed the TFA fertilizer JV with Asociación de Cooperativas Argentinas.

Bunge holds a 30% equity stake in Agrofel Grãos e Insumos, a Brazilian agricultural inputs reseller (fertilizers and crop inputs) that complements its soybean origination business, disclosed in the FY2024 10-K.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume percentages by country for any commodity — Bunge's 10-K reports financial segments regionally (Ag Services & Oilseeds, Refined & Specialty Oils, Milling, Sugar & Bioenergy) but not tonnage by country of origin.

Bunge-Viterra business combination closed July 2, 2025: this adds material grain handling and softseed (canola, wheat) origination assets in Canada, Australia, Europe and Argentina that are NOT yet reflected in the FY2024 10-K-sourced footprint here. Post-merger 10-K (FY2025) will materially expand the country list.

Bunge sold its 50% stake in BP Bunge Bioenergia (Brazilian sugar/ethanol JV) to bp with closing expected Q4 2024; sugar entry above reflects the divestiture but post-2024 Bunge sugar footprint is effectively zero.

Bunge sold its Mexican wheat milling business in Q3 2022 — Mexico no longer a wheat processing-origin country for Bunge; only corn milling remained, and that is also being sold (Apr 2025 agreement with Grain Craft).
Bunge exited its Morocco phosphate fertilizer JV in 2013 — Morocco no longer in scope.
Sunflower origination footprint outside Ukraine (e.g., Romania, where Bunge has consumer oil brands) is not clearly disclosed at the origination-volume level.
Bunge.meta_notes: Bunge is publicly listed on NYSE. The 10-K (FY2024, filed Feb 2025) and the corporate sustainability reporting describe origination, processing, and milling assets country-by-country. Bunge redomesticated from Bermuda to Switzerland on Oct 31, 2023. The Bunge-Viterra business combination closed July 2, 2025 — that adds substantial wheat / canola / grain handling assets in Canada, Australia, and additional Europe/Argentina capacity that are NOT yet reflected in the FY2024 10-K disclosed footprint. As with other traders, exact tonnage by country-of-origin is generally not disclosed.

country_claims.evidence: Cargill publishes a Brazilian soy traceability dashboard and committed to a Soy Moratorium covering the Amazon biome; the Cerrado is publicly named as a critical sourcing area.
Cargill operates major soybean crushing capacity in Argentina (Villa Gobernador Galvez complex) and publishes Argentine soy as part of its origin portfolio.
Cargill operates ~50 US grain elevators and is one of the largest US soy buyers; disclosed in 10-K of joint-venture partners (Carval, AGT Foods) and Cargill press materials.
Cargill publishes a publicly-searchable palm oil supplier list and mill list, mostly Indonesia + Malaysia. Cargill's NDPE policy specifically addresses Indonesian deforestation.
Same supplier-list disclosure; Cargill operates the Kuantan palm-oil refinery in Pahang.
Cargill is one of the largest cocoa buyers from Côte d'Ivoire; publishes Cocoa Promise progress reports with country-level farmer counts.
Cocoa Promise reports disclose Ghana farmer engagement numbers alongside CIV.
Cargill operates major US wheat-handling capacity through its grain elevator network; consistently identified as a top-3 US wheat exporter.
Cargill announced suspension of new investment in Russia in March 2022 but continued grain trading; exited Russian grain export business in July 2023 per public statement.
Cargill operates 4 major US beef-processing plants (High River AB Canada, Friona TX, Schuyler NE, Dodge City KS, Fort Morgan CO).
High River, Alberta plant is one of Canada's two largest beef processors.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume percentages for any commodity
Maize / corn trading footprint not disclosed at country level despite being a top global commodity for Cargill
Cargill exited some markets (Russia, Venezuela) but the date-stamped state of current sourcing isn't always easy to verify
Cocoa volumes by country: Cargill's annual Cocoa Promise reports give farmer counts but not tonnage by country
Cargill.meta_notes: Cargill is privately held and does not publish country-level customs records. Country mentions below are drawn from Cargill's own ESG/sustainability reports + press releases. Volumes are not disclosed; share percentages are NULL by design.

COFCO.ownership: Wholly-owned overseas agribusiness platform of COFCO Corporation, a Chinese state-owned enterprise. Headquartered in Geneva (COFCO International) with parent COFCO Corp in Beijing. Formed via the 2014 acquisition of Nidera and the 2014/2017 acquisition of Noble Agri (renamed COFCO Agri Ltd). Sister entity COFCO Joycome (formerly COFCO Meat) and HK-listed COFCO Joycome Foods (0506.HK) are separate. COFCO Agri-Food / China Agri-Industries Holdings (0606.HK) is the domestic crush/biofuel/milling arm.
COFCO.hq: Geneva, Switzerland (COFCO International); Beijing, China (COFCO Corporation parent)

country_claims.evidence: COFCO International reports that 100% of its directly-sourced soy in Brazil has been mapped to farm; in 2019 it had already achieved 100% traceability in 25 priority Cerrado municipalities, with a target for full direct-soy traceability across Brazil. Brazil is COFCO International's flagship soy origin and is anchored by the Rondonópolis (Mato Grosso) crushing complex.

COFCO International operates the Timbúes complex 30 km from Rosario with 2.3 million tonnes/year soybean crush capacity; the Rosario team originates 14.5 million tonnes of agricultural commodities annually including soy, corn, wheat, sunflower seeds and barley. First COFCO soymeal shipment aligned with EUDR requirements loaded from Argentina in 2024. Argentine soy supply is covered by the VISEC (Gran Chaco) traceability platform.

COFCO sources directly from farmers and operates warehouses in Paraguay; barges carry Paraguayan soy down the Hidrovia river system to Argentine/Uruguayan export ports. COFCO has mapped more than 5.2 million hectares of soy farms across Brazil, Argentina, and Paraguay.

COFCO operates warehouses in Uruguay; Uruguayan ports help export soy from the South Cone. Operations in Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay employ 1,600 people across elevators, crushing facilities and ports.

COFCO International signed a 1.5 million-tonne Brazil-China sustainable soybean trade agreement; the company self-describes its supply chain as anchored in China and competing globally. China is the principal destination market for COFCO International soy and the parent COFCO Corp operates the dominant domestic Chinese crush footprint, though specific Chinese facility-level disclosure is in Chinese-language materials.

COFCO International sources soybeans primarily from South America, as well as from the US and South Africa; the US is one of four declared origination regions (US, Brazil, South Cone, Black Sea). St Louis is identified as a US logistics node.

Standerton Oil Mills (a COFCO International company) is the largest soybean crushing facility in South Africa, employing 125 people and processing soy from both COFCO partner farms and third parties into oil and soy meal for animal feed.

COFCO International entered Ukraine in 2014 via the Noble acquisition; today it operates seven strategic Ukrainian locations including a crushing plant, four grain silos along the Dnieper river, and a Kiev office. Nikolaev (Mykolaiv) port on the Black Sea is the export hub from which wheat, barley, corn, sunflower oil and meal are shipped to Europe, Africa, Middle East and Asia.

From Constanta, the biggest port on the Black Sea, COFCO exports wheat, barley, corn, rapeseeds and sunflower seeds worldwide. COFCO has completed a Romania port terminal upgrade and deployed a new Danube River fleet to power Romanian grain flows.
COFCO's Rosario team originates 14.5 million tonnes of agricultural commodities including wheat from across Argentina annually.

In Australia, COFCO International sources and exports wheat, barley and oilseeds. COFCO International Australia is a globally-focused grain origination and trading business based in Melbourne, supplying sorghum, wheat, barley and chickpeas to South East Asia and the Middle East with a focus on China, Japan and India; inland logistics node at Port Kembla.

Australian wheat origination explicitly serves China as a primary destination; parent COFCO Corp is China's dominant state grain trader/miller, though the China-side milling and reserve footprint is disclosed in Chinese-language materials rather than on cofcointernational.com.
COFCO ships wheat, barley, corn and other food commodities from Ukrainian silos via Nikolaev port. The Black Sea sourcing region is one of four declared global origination areas.

COFCO International announced a target to achieve deforestation- and conversion-free soy and corn supply chains by end-2025 covering Brazil; Brazil is named alongside soy as a corn origin in COFCO's emissions-reduction reporting (Scope 3 FLAG: 14% corn reduction vs. 2021 baseline).
COFCO's Rosario team originates 14.5 million tonnes of agricultural commodities including corn across Argentina annually.
COFCO exports corn (alongside wheat, barley, rapeseed and sunflower seeds) from Constanta port worldwide.
The US is one of four declared COFCO International origination regions (US, Brazil, South Cone, Black Sea) for grains and oilseeds.

COFCO International is one of the largest sugar and ethanol producers in Brazil, operating four mills and producing 1.45 million tonnes of sugar in 2020. The company sources sugarcane largely from São Paulo state and manages 189,000 hectares of cane plantations.

COFCO's English-language disclosures cover Australia for wheat/barley/oilseeds but do not explicitly confirm current ongoing Tully Sugar Queensland operations on cofcointernational.com; Tully Sugar acquired in 2011 by COFCO Corp predates COFCO International's current consolidated reporting and was historically reported via the Hong Kong-listed China Agri-Industries entity. Listed here only for completeness — evidence on cofcointernational.com itself is weak.

Brazil is one of COFCO International's most important coffee growing countries. The company runs its own Coffee Responsible Origin Programme in Brazil engaging some 268 farms, with around 15% of its Brazilian volume sourced from the programme area; 36% of Brazilian coffee sales were responsibly sourced in 2021.
COFCO International collaborates with coffee farmers in Vietnam alongside Brazil, Colombia and Indonesia, and supports independent certification schemes in Vietnam.
COFCO International collaborates with coffee farmers in Colombia and supports independent certification schemes in the country alongside Vietnam.
COFCO International collaborates with coffee farmers in Indonesia alongside Brazil, Vietnam and Colombia.

COFCO International's cotton operations are headquartered in Memphis, Tennessee, and operate two US storage facilities; COFCO buys US cotton bales from ginners and farmers and is increasing sustainability efforts including participation in the US Cotton Trust Protocol.
59% of COFCO International's 2024 Brazilian cotton crop was produced under certification schemes; Brazilian cotton is a disclosed sourcing origin on the products page.

COFCO International continues to source certified Cotton Made in Africa from West Africa; Côte d'Ivoire is the principal West Africa cotton origin under that label and is named in COFCO's regenerative-agriculture engagement via Regenagri partnerships.

evidence_gaps: No share-of-volume percentages disclosed for any commodity by country. COFCO publishes total tonnage (108 million tonnes in 2024) and revenue (US$38.5bn) but does not break down country-of-origin volumes in English-language materials.

MAJOR TRANSPARENCY GAP — China-domestic footprint: COFCO Corporation is the dominant Chinese state grain/oilseed trader, miller and reserve manager, with >200 crush plants, national rice and wheat reserves, and a vast oilseed processing network. None of this is granularly disclosed on cofcointernational.com — it sits with the Beijing parent and HK-listed China Agri-Industries (0606.HK) / COFCO Joycome (0506.HK), and most filings are in Chinese. This file therefore captures only the international-flow side of China (Brazil-to-China soy, Australia-to-China wheat) where it has English-language disclosure.

Russia wheat/grain operations: The 2014 Noble acquisition gave COFCO a Russian trading desk and Black Sea book; there is no recent (post-2022) cofcointernational.com disclosure of an active Russian sourcing footprint, so RUS is NOT included pending direct verification.

Tully Sugar (Queensland, Australia): historically COFCO Corp-owned via the 2011 acquisition, but cofcointernational.com does not currently confirm Tully as part of COFCO International's sugar footprint; included with 'weak' evidence rather than excluded.

Rice trading: Parent COFCO Corp is China's largest rice trader and a major importer from Thailand/Vietnam/Pakistan, but COFCO International's English-language site does not disclose a rice sourcing footprint at country level; rice therefore excluded from this version.

Coffee Africa origins (Ethiopia, Uganda, etc.) acquired with the Noble coffee book are not explicitly named on the current COFCO International coffee page; the four named origins (Brazil, Vietnam, Colombia, Indonesia) are the disclosed set.
Sunflower oil/seed origination: Ukraine and Romania are confirmed; smaller Black Sea origins (Bulgaria, Moldova) are mentioned only generically in COFCO 'around the world' stories.

Sustainability Report 2024 PDF ('Transforming ambitions into reality', cofco-sr24, June 2025) contains additional country-level commentary that could tighten evidence-strength on several countries above — not all of its detail is reflected here in this pass.
COFCO.meta_notes: COFCO publishes an English-language Sustainability Report on cofcointernational.com (latest: 2024 Sustainability Report, 'Transforming ambitions into reality', published June 2025). Country-level commentary on this site is the strongest English-language disclosure available. The parent COFCO Corporation's domestic Chinese footprint (crush, milling, state rice/wheat reserves) is materially larger than COFCO International's footprint but is disclosed almost entirely in Chinese; this file therefore treats China as a strong-evidence sourcing country only where the international/English materials explicitly say so (Brazil-to-China soy flows, China-anchored supply chain), and otherwise flags China as a transparency gap. COFCO International publicly disclosed in 2024 that it handled 108 million tonnes of commodities across 36 countries on six continents with US$38.5bn of revenue. Country tonnage shares by commodity are not disclosed; share_pct is NULL throughout.

JBS.ownership: public; dual-listed June 2025 (NYSE: JBS / B3: JBSS32 BDRs). JBS S.A. continues to trade on B3 as JBSS3. Pilgrim's Pride Corporation (NASDAQ: PPC) is a majority-owned subsidiary (JBS owns ~80% of PPC).
JBS.hq: Amstelveen, Netherlands (JBS N.V. registered HQ) / São Paulo, Brazil (JBS S.A. operational HQ)

country_claims.evidence: JBS S.A. operates the Friboi beef business in Brazil with the country's largest cattle processing footprint. JBS analyses more than 85,000 Brazilian cattle suppliers daily using satellite imagery, farm geo-referencing, and government data; ~15,000 supplier farms have been suspended for non-compliance. JBS has committed to 100% individual cattle traceability in the state of Pará by 2026 (announced at COP28) and operates four Green Offices in Pará (Marabá, Redenção, Santana do Araguaia, Tucumã). From 1 January 2026, JBS direct suppliers must join the Transparent Livestock Platform (PPT) and provide indirect-supplier data.

JBS USA Beef operates nine US beef processing facilities split into four fed-beef plants near major cattle production centres (Cactus TX, Grand Island NE, Greeley CO, Hyrum UT among them) and five regional plants. The Cactus, TX plant employs 3,700+ team members and pays $2.9B/year for livestock; Grand Island, NE processes 1.4M+ cattle per year and exports to 20 countries. A $150M expansion was announced for Cactus.

JBS Foods Canada operates a beef processing facility in Brooks, Alberta with 2,800+ team members, processing ~4,200 head/day and supporting 500+ local cattle producers, with annual livestock payments of CAD 1.6B. A ~$90M expansion is adding a patty processing line and fulfilment centre under Canada's Agri-Processing Investment Tax Credit. JBS Foods Canada also operates Mountain Creek Farms in Calgary and a Belleville, Ontario plant.

JBS Australia operates 11 strategically located production facilities and six feedlots from northern Queensland to Tasmania, with weekly processing capacity of 50,000+ cattle, 80,000+ small stock and 20,000+ hogs. The Dinmore beef plant near Ipswich, Qld is the largest beef facility in the southern hemisphere — 3,400 cattle/day, ~2,000 employees, ~10% of Australia's total beef processing capacity. JBS Australia's Northern Division has five processing plants and five feedlots in QLD/NSW; Beef City (Toowoomba) operates a bioenergy system.

JBS Australia operates four beef and lamb processing facilities and one feedlot across New South Wales, Victoria, Tasmania and South Australia. JBS invested ~A$20M to restart the Cobram (Vic) lamb processing facility, citing increasing demand for Australian lamb, mutton and goat for domestic and international consumers. Brooklyn (Vic), Bordertown (SA), King Island (Tas) and Scone (NSW) are also Southern Division lamb/beef sites.

Pilgrim's Pride is one of the world's largest chicken producers. Pilgrim's operates protein processing and prepared-foods facilities across 14 US states and Puerto Rico. Approximately 60,000 employees globally across all Pilgrim's businesses. Pilgrim's US is the largest of four Pilgrim's business units (Pilgrim's US, Pilgrim's Mexico, Pilgrim's Moy Park, Pilgrim's UK).

Pilgrim's Mexico operates as a major chicken producer through subsidiaries including Avícola Pilgrim's Pride de Mexico, FAMPAT, Gallina Pesada, Inmobiliaria Avicola Pilgrim's Pride, Pilgrim's Pride S. de R.L., Plan Pro Restaurantes, and Servicios Administrativos Pilgrim's Pride per Pilgrim's Pride FY2024 10-K Exhibit 21 subsidiary list.

Seara, a JBS S.A. subsidiary, runs vertically-integrated chicken (and pork) operations in Brazil. The Seara segment includes chicken/pork processing, value-added products, and prepared foods including the Incrível plant-based brand. Seara is one of Brazil's largest poultry exporters.

Pilgrim's Europe (Pilgrim's Moy Park + Pilgrim's UK + Pilgrim's Food Masters) operates 40+ facilities across the UK, Ireland, France and the Netherlands with 17,000+ employees. Moy Park is a leading UK poultry and prepared-foods supplier; Pilgrim's UK is described as the UK's number one pig farmer and higher-welfare pork producer. Moy Park was acquired by Pilgrim's from JBS S.A. for $1.3B (£1.0B) in 2017.

Moy Park has poultry operations in the Republic of Ireland. Pilgrim's Pride 10-K Exhibit 21 lists Irish subsidiaries: Arkose Investments Unlimited Company, Consumer Foods Van Sales (Ireland) Ltd., Pilgrim's Food Masters Ireland Ltd., and Rye Valley Foods Ltd.

Pilgrim's Europe operations include facilities in France via Pilgrim's Food Masters (chilled and frozen ready meals). Pilgrim's groups 40+ European facilities across UK, Ireland, France and the Netherlands.
Pilgrim's Europe operates in the Netherlands via Pilgrim's Food Masters (chilled and frozen ready meals). JBS N.V., the listed parent, is also registered in Amstelveen, Netherlands.
Pilgrim's Pride operates a prepared-foods facility in Puerto Rico per Pilgrim's Sustainability Report 'About Our Company' chapter.

JBS USA Pork operates five US pork production facilities: Beardstown IL, Louisville KY, Marshalltown IA, Ottumwa IA and Worthington MN. Beardstown employs 1,900+ and pays 265+ hog producers >$810M/year. Worthington processes ~20,000 hogs/day. A national union contract covers Beardstown, Worthington, Marshalltown and Ottumwa. JBS USA also signed an agreement to acquire certain hog assets from TriOak Foods.
Seara operates vertically-integrated pork operations in Brazil alongside chicken — disclosed in JBS S.A.'s investor materials as a single Seara segment covering chicken, pork, prepared foods.

JBS Australia operates Australia's largest smallgoods manufacturer, Primo Foods (ham, bacon, salami, deli meats), and processes hogs at ~20,000/week as part of the broader 50,000 cattle / 80,000 small stock / 20,000 hogs weekly capacity. JBS Australia is also the parent of Rivalea, a major Australian pork producer (acquired 2022 via the Primo acquisition).
Pilgrim's UK self-describes as 'the UK's number one pig farmer and producer of higher welfare pork.' Pilgrim's Europe page enumerates Pilgrim's UK alongside Moy Park and Pilgrim's Food Masters.
Primo Foods (JBS Australia) is described by the JBS Foods Group as Australia's largest smallgoods manufacturer — ham, bacon, salami and deli meats sold to retail and foodservice across Australia.

JBS Australia oversees Premier Beehive in New Zealand, described as New Zealand's number one bacon and ham company. JBS Australia also owns a controlling stake in Scott Technology, an NZ-headquartered automation/robotics developer for meat processors.

Planterra Foods (JBS USA's plant-based subsidiary, Colorado-based) launched the OZO brand of pea-and-rice-protein products fermented by shiitake mushrooms — 22g protein per serving — into US retail and DTC e-commerce.
Seara's Incrível brand is JBS's Brazilian plant-based platform; included in JBS S.A. brand portfolio and Seara segment disclosures.
JBS acquired Vivera (Netherlands), described by JBS as 'the third largest producer of plant-based products in Europe,' to expand its global plant-based food platform.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume or tonnage percentages by country for any commodity — JBS discloses facility counts, head/day or week, supplier counts and payroll figures, not country-level revenue or tonnage shares.
JBS Leather (hides) global sourcing footprint not researched in this pass — JBS operates a separate Leather business across BRA / USA / Europe but country-level sourcing is not enumerated here.

JBS Friboi indirect cattle supplier monitoring: JBS commits all direct suppliers to onboard the Transparent Livestock Platform by 1 Jan 2026 and to provide indirect-supplier data; the actual % of indirect suppliers covered (vs direct) is still in transition and not stated as a single number in JBS's own materials.
Brazilian beef supply chain coverage by biome — JBS publishes Amazon-biome cattle monitoring (~85,000 suppliers analysed daily) but Cerrado/Pantanal coverage is less granularly disclosed.

USDA-regulated US-imported lean trimmings or Canadian/Mexican beef cross-flows between JBS plants are not disclosed in country-of-origin sourcing terms on the public site (they are inter-affiliate plant flows).

Argentine / Paraguayan / Uruguayan JBS beef operations: JBS historically had South American beef plants beyond Brazil (e.g., the former Swift Argentina, Paraguay plants). Their current operating status in JBS's 2024 disclosures was not freshly verified in this research pass and is therefore omitted to avoid stale claims.

Sustainability reports prior to FY2024 referenced (sustainability.jbsfoodsgroup.com) — the URL appears stable but specific page citations rely on the live nav state; a fresh fetch is recommended before publishing for production use.

Brief flagged a possible 'JBS Smithfield acquisition' — this is NOT supported by JBS's own materials. Smithfield Foods is a WH Group subsidiary. The Smithfield claim is excluded from this dataset; consider re-checking the brief if a different acquisition was intended (e.g., specific Smithfield plant divestitures sold to JBS — none verified here).

Pilgrim's Europe France/Netherlands operations: Pilgrim's Food Masters operates across UK/IRL/FR/NL but the site does not enumerate site-by-site facility counts per country in publicly-indexed pages used for this pass.

JBS NV (the new NYSE-listed parent, ticker JBS, listed June 2025) Form 20-F was filed in early 2026 covering FY2025 — full segment-by-country revenue breakdown is in that filing but was not parsed line-by-line in this research pass.
JBS.meta_notes: World's largest meat processor by revenue. Operates four core business platforms: (1) Beef — JBS Brasil (Friboi brand), JBS USA Beef, JBS Foods Canada, JBS Australia (incl. Primo Foods smallgoods, Premier Beehive NZ); (2) Poultry — Seara (Brazil), Pilgrim's Pride (USA / Mexico / Puerto Rico) and Pilgrim's Europe (Moy Park UK+IRL, Pilgrim's UK, Pilgrim's Food Masters UK/IRL/FR/NL); (3) Pork — JBS USA Pork (Swift brand), Seara (Brazil), JBS Australia (Primo), Pilgrim's UK; (4) Prepared / value-added foods + plant-based (Incrível by Seara in Brazil; OZO by Planterra in the US; Vivera in Europe). Country claims below are tagged to the JBS operating entity for each. Brazil cattle traceability disclosures are exceptional vs peers — JBS publishes the Transparent Livestock Platform (PPT) and a public socio-environmental compliance dashboard for direct cattle suppliers in the Amazon biome and has committed to 100% individual animal traceability in Pará by 2026. Share-of-volume percentages by country are not disclosed in JBS's own materials and are NULL by design. IMPORTANT — the user-task brief flagged a possible 'Smithfield acquisition' for verification; that claim does NOT appear in JBS's own materials. Smithfield Foods is owned by WH Group, not JBS. The Smithfield claim is therefore EXCLUDED from this dataset.

Louis Dreyfus.ownership: private (Louis-Dreyfus family via Akira Holding; Margarita Louis-Dreyfus controlling shareholder; minority stake held by Abu Dhabi Developmental Holding Co. — ADQ — since 2020)
Louis Dreyfus.hq: Rotterdam, Netherlands (registered HQ) / Geneva, Switzerland (operating HQ)

country_claims.evidence: LDC Ukraine self-describes as one of the country's three largest grains & oilseeds exporters, specialising in wheat, barley and maize. LDC operates a multi-commodity Odesa port terminal on the Black Sea (250,000 MT grain storage, 4-5 Mt/yr export turnover) and the Haisyn grains & oilseeds warehouse complex (57,000 MT) in Vinnytsia region.

LDC Russia page describes LDC as 'a leading grains exporter in Russia,' focused on wheat, barley and corn, with ~900,000 MT of inland grains storage and a 50 KMT Azov terminal. (Note: LDC, like other Western traders, has been under scrutiny over Russian operations; current disclosure status on country page reflects the most recent ldc.com text.)

In Argentina, LDC specialises in exporting surplus wheat, corn, sorghum and barley to global markets, with 20+ transport, 30+ processing and 70+ storage facilities. LDC has been operating in Argentina since 1897 and is the country's third-largest exporter.
LDC's North America Grains & Oilseeds Platform is anchored in the US; LDC operates origination, processing and logistics assets across the US for grains and oilseeds.

LDC is one of the largest soy traders in Brazil; the company achieved 100% soy traceability to farm level for direct suppliers in high-priority Cerrado municipalities under WBCSD Soft Commodities Forum methodology, expanding from 25 to 61 focus municipalities covering >70% of Cerrado land-conversion risk. LDC has integrated soy crushing and origination assets in Brazil and is building a greenfield fertiliser facility in Rondonópolis.

LDC operates two agro-industrial complexes in Santa Fe province — Timbúes (handles >4 Mt of oilseeds annually) and General Lagos (2.5 Mt/yr soybean crush, 600,000 t/yr biodiesel — the world's largest soybean-based biodiesel plant, plus 30,000 t lecithin and 60,000 t glycerin per year) — plus Bahía Blanca in Buenos Aires province.

Nutrien.hq: Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada (registered head office); Calgary, Alberta (corporate office)

country_claims.evidence: Nutrien's Potash segment mines and processes potash at six Saskatchewan mines: Allan, Cory, Lanigan, Patience Lake, Rocanville and Vanscoy. The Saskatchewan Ministry of Energy and Resources has granted Nutrien the exclusive right to mine potash on approximately 394,000 hectares of Crown land. As of December 31, 2025, Nutrien estimates its Potash segment represents approximately 20 percent of global potash nameplate capacity. Offshore exports flow through Canpotex marine terminals at Vancouver, BC; Saint John, NB; Portland, OR; and Thunder Bay, ON.

Nutrien operates four Canadian nitrogen plants: Carseland, Alberta (ammonia, urea, ESN); Fort Saskatchewan, Alberta (ammonia, urea); Joffre, Alberta (ammonia); and Redwater, Alberta (ammonia, urea, urea solutions, nitric acid, ammonium nitrate, UAN, ammonium sulfate). The Redwater facility has been capturing and exporting CO2 into the Alberta Carbon Trunk Line since late 2019, sending ~274,000 tonnes of CO2 in 2025.

Nutrien operates four US nitrogen plants: Augusta, Georgia (ammonia, urea, UAN, urea solutions, nitric acid, ammonium nitrate); Borger, Texas (ammonia, urea, urea solutions); Geismar, Louisiana (ammonia, urea, UAN, urea solutions, nitric acid); and Lima, Ohio (ammonia, urea, UAN, urea solutions, nitric acid, ammonium nitrate). Nutrien decided in 2024 to no longer pursue the Geismar Clean Ammonia project.

Nutrien operates the Point Lisas, Trinidad nitrogen facility (ammonia and urea) via PCS Nitrogen Trinidad Limited (100% owned subsidiary). On October 23, 2025, Nutrien completed a controlled shutdown of the Trinidad facility due to uncertainty with respect to port access and a lack of reliable and economic gas supply; operations remain in controlled shutdown as Nutrien assesses long-term options. Prior to shutdown, nitrogen products from Trinidad were distributed primarily to markets in the US, South America and Europe.

Nutrien operates two integrated phosphate mine-and-processing complexes in the US: Aurora, North Carolina (MAP, purified phosphoric acid, ammonium phosphate; 2025 phosphate rock production 4.66 million tonnes) and White Springs, Florida (MAP, MAP+MST, MGA, SPA, LOMAG, POLY; 2025 phosphate rock production 1.39 million tonnes). Most offshore phosphate sales ship via the Morehead City, NC terminal. Ammonia feedstock is supplied from Nutrien's Lima OH and Augusta GA nitrogen plants. In 2025 Phosphate sales to third parties represented 6 percent of consolidated revenue. Nutrien initiated a review of strategic alternatives for its Phosphate business in Q4 2025, which may include reconfiguring operations, strategic partnerships or a potential sale of all or part of the business. The Conda, Idaho phosphate production facility was divested in 2018 but Nutrien retains environmental liabilities; the former Geismar, Louisiana phosphoric acid facility settled with EPA/LDEQ in October 2022.

Nutrien Ag Solutions operates approximately 1,800 retail locations across North America, Australia and South America serving farmers in seven countries (US, Canada, Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Chile, Uruguay). Nutrien states it is the largest global ag retailer with >4,200 crop consultants and >600,000 customer accounts worldwide. The US is Nutrien Ag Solutions' largest single-country market.

Nutrien Ag Solutions (Canada) Inc. is a wholly-owned subsidiary disclosed in the 2025 40-F Annual Information Form. Nutrien Ag Solutions Canada operates a national retail network for crop nutrients, crop protection, seed and services.

Nutrien Ag Solutions Limited (Western Australia, Australia) is a wholly-owned subsidiary disclosed in the 2025 40-F Annual Information Form. Operates as Landmark / Nutrien Ag Solutions across Australia; Australian operations also include merchandise sales.

Nutrien Ag Solutions operates in Brazil, but the footprint has been materially reduced. Per the 2025 40-F, Nutrien 'closed more than 50 locations and suspended operations at all blending facilities in Brazil' in line with its strategic priority to exit non-core South American Retail activities, and 'has since also initiated the divestiture of idle facilities in Brazil.' Impairments of US$335 million (2024) and US$465 million (2023) were taken on Brazil Retail property, plant and equipment.

Nutrien Ag Solutions operates a retail presence in Argentina (nutrienagsolutions.com.ar). Separately, Nutrien divested its 50 percent equity position in Profertil S.A. (Argentine ammonia/urea JV with YPF S.A.) in 2025 for US$595 million gross proceeds, exiting Argentine nitrogen production but retaining the Argentine retail distribution business.
Nutrien Ag Solutions operates a Chilean retail presence (nutrienagsolutions.cl), one of seven countries where Nutrien's retail network operates according to the corporate website.
Nutrien Ag Solutions operates a Uruguayan retail presence (nutrienagsolutions.com.uy), one of seven countries where Nutrien's retail network operates according to the corporate website.

evidence_gaps: Nutrien states it sold approximately 27.5 million metric tonnes of manufactured fertilizer to customers in 'more than 50 countries' in 2025, but does not publish a country-of-destination breakdown for fertilizer sales. Country claims here therefore reflect documented production locations and disclosed retail-country presence only.

Potash exports outside the US and Canada are handled through Canpotex (a marketing/logistics JV with The Mosaic Company); Canpotex is the exclusive offshore sales channel for Nutrien's Canadian potash but published destination-country volumes are aggregate (Canpotex level), not split by Nutrien share, so destination countries are not enumerated in this file.
Trinidad nitrogen production is currently in controlled shutdown (effective October 23, 2025); future production status depends on Nutrien's ongoing strategic review and remains uncertain.
Phosphate business is under strategic review (reconfiguration, partnership or sale) as initiated in Q4 2025; US production footprint at Aurora NC and White Springs FL may change materially.

Brazil Retail footprint has been substantially reduced (50+ locations closed; blending operations suspended; idle-facility divestiture initiated) but Nutrien has not exited Brazil entirely; remaining footprint is not quantified in the 40-F.
Profertil S.A. (Argentina nitrogen JV, 50% stake) was divested in 2025; Argentina is no longer a nitrogen production country for Nutrien but remains a retail country.
Sinofert Holdings Limited (China potash distribution) was fully divested in 2025; China is not included as a distribution country.
The Conda, Idaho phosphate facility was divested in 2018; Idaho is not included as a current production state.
Nutrien.meta_notes: Nutrien Ltd. was formed January 1, 2018 from the merger of Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan (PotashCorp) and Agrium Inc. It is the world's largest fertilizer producer by capacity and the largest global agricultural retailer. Nutrien is a Canadian foreign private issuer; it files Form 40-F with the SEC (annual report) rather than 10-K. Nutrien is NOT a grain trader: its business is (a) upstream fertilizer manufacturing (Potash, Nitrogen, Phosphate) and (b) downstream agricultural retail distribution (Nutrien Ag Solutions). Country claims below distinguish PRODUCTION (mining/manufacturing) from DISTRIBUTION (retail/sales) footprint. Several material divestitures and shutdowns are reflected: Profertil S.A. (Argentine nitrogen JV, 50% stake) sold in 2025 for US$595M; Sinofert Holdings (China potash distributor) fully divested 2025; Trinidad Nitrogen facility entered controlled shutdown October 23, 2025; Phosphate business is under strategic review (possible reconfiguration, partnership or sale) as of Q4 2025; Brazil Retail closed 50+ locations and suspended all blending operations in 2024–2025 and initiated divestiture of idle facilities. The Conda, Idaho phosphate facility was divested in 2018. Nutrien states it sells to customers in 'more than 50 countries' but does not publish a per-country sales breakdown; therefore this file restricts country claims to documented mining/manufacturing locations and disclosed retail-country presence.
