Olam Group.meta_notes: IMPORTANT — 2022-2026 restructure. Olam Group's re-organisation plan split the original company into three operating units: (1) Olam Food Ingredients ('ofi') — cocoa, coffee, dairy, nuts, spices — which has its own management and is being separately listed; (2) Olam Agri — grains & oilseeds, wheat milling & pasta, rice, edible oils, animal feed & protein, cotton, wood products, rubber, sugar — now majority 80.01% owned by Saudi Agricultural & Livestock Investment Company (SALIC) following Tranche 1 closing in April 2026, with Olam Group retaining a 19.99% associated-company stake; (3) the Remaining Olam Group. Each country claim below is tagged with the operating entity (ofi vs Olam Agri vs Olam Group) so the entity-of-record is unambiguous. Olam's AtSource sustainability insights platform and OFIS (Olam Farmer Information System) feed traceability data primarily for ofi commodities (cocoa, coffee, nuts). Country tonnage shares are generally not disclosed in Olam's own materials; share_pct is therefore NULL across the board.

Tyson Foods.ownership: public (NYSE: TSN). Tyson family retains voting control via Class B super-voting shares (Tyson Limited Partnership).
commodities: Prepared / further-processed meats (bacon, sausage, lunchmeat, hot dogs, ready meals)
Cattle, soy, palm oil, pulp/paper/packaging (Forest Protection Standard scope)

country_claims.evidence: Tyson Fresh Meats operates six US beef plants: Amarillo TX, Dakota City NE, Finney County KS (Holcomb), Joslin IL, Lexington NE, and Pasco WA. Dakota City NE is one of the largest US beef plants — Tyson's own press release describes its 4,300-person workforce producing 'enough beef in one day to feed 18 million people.' A $47M expansion of the Lexington NE boxed-beef warehouse was announced. Tyson's FY2024 10-K (period ended Sep 28 2024) discloses sales of products produced in a country other than the US were less than 10% of consolidated sales, so the Beef segment is overwhelmingly US-origin.

Tyson Fresh Meats operates US pork plants in Storm Lake IA, Logansport IN, Council Bluffs IA, Madison NE, and additional Midwestern sites. Tyson's own 'Tyson Foods Major Locations' document and press releases enumerate Storm Lake (Iowa), Logansport (Indiana) and Madison (Nebraska — fresh pork + ham operations) as core pork facilities; a Council Bluffs IA case-ready beef+pork plant added 60 jobs in 2015. Per the FY2024 10-K, less than 10% of consolidated sales were produced outside the US — so the Pork segment is essentially 100% US.

Tyson operates a vertically-integrated US poultry production process (hatcheries, broiler farms, feed mills, processing plants) — headquartered in Springdale AR with ~23,000 employees in Arkansas alone, plus plants across GA, AL, KY (Albany + Robards), TN (a $425M new Tennessee complex announced in 2021), MS, TX, NC, VA. The Springdale Incubation Technology Center (75,000 sq ft) supplies chicks for NW Arkansas broiler and Cornish hen operations. The Pine Bluff AR plant (built 1991) produces fully cooked chicken.

Tyson de Mexico is described by Tyson Foods as 'a vertically-integrated poultry production company' with offices in Mexico City, distributing poultry, pork and beef to retail, foodservice and wholesale customers in Mexico. Tyson Foods' 'A Global Food Company' page on tysonfoods.com enumerates the Mexican subsidiary.

Tyson Poultry Thailand Ltd., established in 1991, is described by Tyson Foods as 'one of the country's largest fully integrated chicken processors and a leader in manufacturing fully cooked chicken products worldwide.' Tyson then expanded its Thai footprint in June 2019 by completing the $340M acquisition of BRF S.A.'s Thai and European operations — four additional Thailand processing plants (with feed mill, hatchery, breeder farms and contract grower base) producing fresh and frozen value-added raw and fully-cooked poultry for retail and foodservice customers across Asia and Europe. Brands include Grabits, Hot 'N' Kickin' Chicken, Speedy Pollo, and Sadia.

Tyson established its first Chinese factory in 2001. In November 2018, Tyson completed the $2.16B acquisition of Keystone Foods from Marfrig Global Foods — bringing eight plants and three innovation centers across China, South Korea, Malaysia, Thailand and Australia. In November 2020, Tyson Foods announced plans for additional production facilities for its International business, including a new plant in China expected to create more than 700 jobs.

The 2018 Keystone Foods acquisition press release explicitly lists Malaysia among the five countries (China, South Korea, Malaysia, Thailand, Australia) housing Keystone's eight plants and three innovation centers. Tyson's subsequent international expansion announcements (Nov 2020) reaffirm Malaysia as an operating market.

The 2018 Keystone Foods acquisition press release names South Korea among the five Asia-Pacific countries where the acquired plants and innovation centers were located (China, South Korea, Malaysia, Thailand, Australia).

Australia entered Tyson's footprint via the 2018 Keystone Foods acquisition. Tyson's 'A Global Food Company' page describes an Australian operation originally established 1997 in the Brisbane Valley as a purpose-built beef-patty manufacturer servicing major QSR customers in Australia. Tyson Australia is part of Tyson's Asia Pacific business.

Tyson Foods' June 2019 completion of the BRF S.A. acquisition included 'one processing facility in the Netherlands' alongside one in the UK and four in Thailand. In November 2020 Tyson announced plans to expand its Netherlands facility as part of an international growth push.

Tyson Foods' June 2019 BRF acquisition included 'one processing facility in the United Kingdom' producing further-processed chicken under Grabits, Hot 'N' Kickin' Chicken, Speedy Pollo, Sadia and customer-owned brands for European retail and foodservice customers.

country_claims.commodity: Prepared / further-processed meats (bacon, sausage, lunchmeat, hot dogs, ready meals)
country_claims.evidence: Tyson's Prepared Foods segment produces meals under Tyson, Jimmy Dean, Hillshire Farm, Ball Park, Wright, Aidells, ibp and State Fair brands following the 2014 Hillshire Brands acquisition. Tyson invested $180M to add seven production lines and 270,000 sq ft of capacity at Caseyville IL for Hillshire Farm and Jimmy Dean output, creating ~400 jobs. Tyson built a new state-of-the-art bacon plant in Bowling Green KY (production began late 2023, 450 jobs) for Jimmy Dean and Wright Brand bacon, plus additional Kentucky prepared-foods operations in Claryville. A new fully-cooked food production plant opened in Virginia in November 2023.

Tyson's sustainability disclosures describe the commodities and ingredients needed for day-to-day operations as 'wheat, corn, rice, soy, dairy and vegetables.' Tyson's Local Grain Services (LGS) program is described on Tyson's own ESG hub as a way to 'actively build resources for and relationships with local grain farmers who provide corn for [Tyson's] birds, to meet our commitment to use grain locally within our supply chain.' US Midwest corn + soy is the dominant feedstock for Tyson's US broiler operations.

country_claims.commodity: Cattle, soy, palm oil, pulp/paper/packaging (Forest Protection Standard scope)
country_claims.evidence: Tyson's November 2020 Forest Protection Standard targets four commodities — cattle/beef, soy, palm oil, and pulp/paper/packaging — globally. Tyson commissioned a Proforest deforestation-risk assessment which concluded 94% of Tyson's land footprint is no-to-low risk of deforestation, ~3% is medium-to-high risk, and ~3% has unidentified sourcing origin. Country-level mill or supplier lists for palm oil are not published by Tyson in the same granularity as peers, so country-level palm oil sourcing claims are NOT made here — the Forest Protection Standard is included only as a global commitment.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume or tonnage percentages by country for any commodity. Tyson's FY2024 10-K only tells us non-US-produced sales are <10% of consolidated sales — it does not break out country shares within the international tail.

Tyson does not publish a country-level palm-oil supplier or mill list comparable to Cargill's NDPE disclosure; the Forest Protection Standard names palm oil as in-scope but origin geographies (Indonesia/Malaysia) are not specifically claimed in Tyson's own materials and are therefore NOT enumerated here.

Tyson's soy sourcing is not disclosed at country level. The Local Grain Services program is described as US-corn-focused; whether Tyson sources any soy outside the US (e.g., from Brazil or Argentina) is not stated in Tyson's own published materials.

Keystone Foods sub-country plant counts: Tyson's 2018 acquisition press release confirms 'eight plants and three innovation centers' across China + S. Korea + Malaysia + Thailand + Australia but does not enumerate plants per country in a single public document. Per-country plant counts are therefore omitted to avoid stale or inferred claims.

South Korea and Malaysia footprint detail is light — Tyson's own pages confirm the countries are within the Keystone perimeter but do not describe plant locations, capacity, or product mix at country level.

Tyson Australia: Tyson's 'A Global Food Company' page references three Australian facilities and 1,500+ employees in Asia Pacific (not Australia alone) — facility-level details (Brisbane Valley beef-patty plant only) are sparse.

Brazil-origin chicken: the Sadia brand is now Tyson-owned for non-Brazil markets (acquired via BRF deal), but Tyson does NOT operate poultry production in Brazil itself — Sadia in Brazil remains a BRF brand. This dataset therefore makes no Brazil claim for Tyson.

Cattle traceability: unlike JBS Brazil, Tyson does not publish a public cattle-supplier traceability dashboard for its US fed-cattle supply chain; the Forest Protection Standard sets a deforestation-risk goal but supplier-level disclosures are not country-segmented.
Russia / European Union beyond NL/UK: Tyson does not list operations elsewhere in continental Europe in its own materials; the EU footprint is limited to the Netherlands plant from the BRF deal.

FY2024 10-K (filed Nov 2024, period ended Sep 28 2024) is the latest annual filing referenced here; FY2025 10-K (period ended ~late Sep 2025) would be Tyson's most recent annual SEC filing as of May 2026 but specific FY2025 segment-by-country revenue lines were not parsed in this research pass.
Tyson Foods.meta_notes: Tyson Foods is the largest US meat processor by revenue and operates four reportable segments: Beef, Pork, Chicken, and Prepared Foods, plus an International/Other category. In its FY2024 Form 10-K (period ended Sep 28 2024), Tyson disclosed that 'sales of products produced in a country other than the United States were less than 10% of consolidated sales' — i.e. ~90%+ of production is US-based. The international footprint flows mainly through (a) Tyson de Mexico (vertically-integrated chicken, offices in Mexico City), (b) Tyson Poultry Thailand Ltd. (est. 1991) plus four further plants acquired from BRF S.A. in June 2019, (c) China operations dating to 2001 and the 2018 Keystone Foods acquisition (Tyson Foods bought Keystone from Marfrig for $2.16B; that deal brought eight plants + three innovation centers across China, South Korea, Malaysia, Thailand and Australia), and (d) European plants in the Netherlands and United Kingdom acquired from BRF. US production is dominated by Tyson Fresh Meats (beef: Amarillo TX, Dakota City NE, Finney County KS, Joslin IL, Lexington NE, Pasco WA; pork: Storm Lake IA, Logansport IN, Council Bluffs IA, Madison NE, Columbus Junction IA, Waterloo IA, Perry IA), the Chicken segment (Springdale AR HQ + complexes across AR, GA, AL, KY, TN, MS, TX, NC, VA), and Prepared Foods (Hillshire Farm / Jimmy Dean / Ball Park / Wright / Aidells brands acquired via the 2014 Hillshire Brands deal; key sites include Caseyville IL and Bowling Green KY). Share-of-volume percentages by country are NOT published in Tyson's own materials and are NULL by design. Tyson's 2024 sustainability disclosures focus on a Forest Protection Standard for four priority commodities (cattle/beef, soy, palm oil, pulp/paper/packaging); a Proforest deforestation-risk assessment concluded 94% of Tyson's land footprint is no-to-low risk.

Viterra.ownership: private — ~49.99% Glencore plc, ~40% Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPPIB), ~9% British Columbia Investment Management Corporation (BCI), remainder Viterra Employee Benefit Trust (VEBT)
Viterra.hq: Rotterdam, Netherlands (international HQ) + Regina, Saskatchewan (Canadian operations, 'Viterra Inc.')

country_claims.evidence: Viterra Canada (HQ Regina, Saskatchewan) operated a network of 66 licensed primary grain elevators across Western Canada (Saskatchewan, Alberta, Manitoba) plus port terminals at Vancouver, Prince Rupert, Thunder Bay, Montreal and Quebec City; wheat was the largest single commodity in the Canadian grain handling business that traces back to the Saskatchewan Wheat Pool.

Viterra Australia operated 58 storage sites in southern Western Australia, South Australia and western Victoria with 10+ million tonnes of storage capacity, plus six bulk grain export terminals in South Australia (Port Adelaide Outer Harbor, Port Adelaide Inner Harbour, Port Lincoln, Wallaroo, Port Giles and Thevenard); Viterra was one of the largest buyers and exporters of Australian wheat.

Viterra Ukraine operated two modern Black Sea port terminals at Chornomorsk and Mykolaiv handling Ukrainian grain (wheat, corn) and vegetable oil exports; the Mykolaiv oil terminal was struck by Russian forces in 2023.

Viterra owned a grain terminal at Rostov-on-Don and a 50% stake in the Taman deep-water port terminal (the other 50% held by Demetra Holding, 45%-owned by Russian state bank VTB); Viterra announced exit from Russian origination/export on July 1, 2023, but the disposal was structured as a transfer to Russian management (rebranded 'MZK Export') with Viterra B.V. reportedly remaining a key buyer; Viterra continued Russian grain trading materially longer than Cargill, ADM and Bunge had committed to (peer announcements March-April 2023; Viterra confirmed in April 2023). In 2024 the successor MZK Export remained among Russia's top-five grain exporters.

Through the US$1.125 billion acquisition of Gavilon Agriculture (completed October 2022 from Marubeni), Viterra gained 120+ US grain elevators with ~345 million bushels of storage capacity, handling wheat, soybeans and corn across the US grain belt.

Viterra Argentina S.A. (formerly Oleaginosa Moreno, rebranded May 2021) operated grain origination plus a port terminal at Quequén, Buenos Aires; in 2024 Viterra Argentina exported 9.39 million tons of grains and by-products, ranking second nationally behind Cargill.

Viterra had an expanding origination footprint in Brazil; Brazil was named alongside Argentina and Europe as a source of growth in the 2023 Full-Year Report. Combined Bunge+Viterra accounted for ~20.9% of Brazilian soybean exports in 2022.

Viterra was the dominant Canadian canola originator via its Western Canada elevator network and operated the Bécancour, Quebec oilseed processing plant (crushing capacity 3,000 t/day / ~1.05 million t/yr canola + soybean, plus 1,200 t/day refining); in April 2021 Viterra announced an additional integrated canola crush plant in Regina, Saskatchewan with an initial 2.5 million t/yr capacity — described at announcement as the world's largest integrated canola crush facility, targeted to come online late 2024.

Viterra Australia handled both GM and non-GM canola through its South Australian / Victorian storage and port-terminal network; canola was disclosed alongside wheat, barley, pulses, sorghum and cotton as a core commodity.
Rapeseed origination was part of Viterra's Black Sea trade flow through its Ukrainian terminals; pulses/oilseed marketing materials list Black Sea as one of Viterra's main supply regions.

Viterra operated rapeseed and sunflower-seed oilseed crushing and grain origination in Hungary; the European Commission required Bunge to divest Viterra's entire oilseeds business in Hungary as a clearance remedy because of rapeseed/sunflower competition concerns in Central Europe; these assets were subsequently acquired by Louis Dreyfus Company in 2025.
Same EU divestiture: Viterra's entire grains and oilseeds (rapeseed, sunflower) business in Poland was required to be sold to remedy competition concerns; acquired by Louis Dreyfus Company in 2025.

Barley (feed and malting) was a core commodity handled through Viterra's Western Canadian elevator network; the Saskatchewan Wheat Pool / Viterra Inc. lineage made the company a primary buyer of prairie barley.
Feed and malting barley disclosed as a primary commodity at Port Adelaide Inner Harbour and across Viterra Australia's 58-site network; Viterra was one of the largest exporters of Australian barley.

Viterra was described in its own marketing materials as 'one of the world's largest suppliers of seaborne-traded pulses', with the main producing region being Saskatchewan (the world's largest exporter of lentils and dried peas); operations included specialty pulse cleaning plants in Alberta and Saskatchewan.
Australian pulses (faba beans, field peas, lentils) listed as bulk commodities handled at Port Adelaide Inner Harbour.

Viterra operated a sunflower seed and sunflower-oil export terminal at the Black Sea port of Mykolaiv (struck by Russian forces in 2023); Ukraine was a core sunflower origination country and Viterra was a leading global exporter of sunflower oil.

Sunflower seed origination was part of Viterra's pre-2023 Russian flow handled out of Rostov/Taman terminals; same exit/transfer dynamic as wheat (formal exit July 1, 2023; successor MZK Export continued exports).
Viterra Argentina operated industrial sunflower (and soybean) processing plants at Daireaux, Quequén and Ingeniero White; achieved record crushing volumes in 2023.

Viterra held 66.7% of Renova S.A., operator of the Timbúes (Santa Fe) soybean crushing plant on the Paraná river — Viterra described it as the world's largest single-site soybean crushing facility — plus a biodiesel facility at San Lorenzo. Viterra Argentina ranked #2 national grain/oilseed exporter in 2024 (9.39 Mt).

The Gavilon acquisition (Oct 2022) elevated Viterra to ~the third-largest exporter of Brazilian soybeans, ahead of ADM, per Cargonave shipping-agency data; combined Bunge+Viterra represented ~20.9% of Brazilian soybean exports in 2022 (the figure used to justify the merger's market significance).

Gavilon's US grain elevator network (120+ facilities) handled significant US soybean origination volumes; per Reuters/Cargonave commentary on the deal, the Gavilon acquisition gave Glencore/Viterra a much larger US foothold for soybean trading.
The Bécancour, Quebec plant processed both canola and soybeans, drawing soybean supply from eastern Canada (Ontario, Quebec).

Bécancour (Quebec) canola+soy crush and refining + Regina (Saskatchewan) integrated canola crush plant (2.5 Mt/yr) — at announcement positioned as the world's largest integrated canola crush facility, targeted for late-2024 operation.
Renova Timbúes soybean crush + San Lorenzo biodiesel + Daireaux/Quequén/Ingeniero White sunflower+soy crushing complexes.
Mykolaiv sunflower-oil export terminal (struck by Russian missiles in 2023).

evidence_gaps: Viterra was a private company part-owned by Glencore (consolidated into Glencore's IFRS statements) and by CPPIB/BCI; it did not publish a 10-K-equivalent country-by-country asset register. Country lists here are reconstructed from Viterra's own press releases, segment websites (viterra.ca, viterra.com.au, viterraukraine.com.ua, viterra.com.ar), the 2023 Full-Year Report PDF, and contemporaneous trade press. Tonnage by country of origin is not disclosed.

Russia (RUS) post-2022 status is genuinely murky: Viterra publicly announced exit from Russian origination/export effective July 1, 2023, but the disposal was structured as a transfer of 'Viterra Rus' to Russian management (rebranded MZK Export, completed October 2023) with no disclosed sale proceeds. Market intelligence and journalism (Censor.NET; The Globe and Mail; Reuters) indicate Viterra B.V. continued to be a key buyer of MZK Export grain afterwards. In 2024 MZK Export exported ~3.5 Mt and remained a top-five Russian exporter. Viterra continued Russian grain trading materially longer than Cargill, ADM and Bunge had publicly committed to.

EU-mandated divestitures (Hungary, Poland oilseeds + grains) were completed via sale to Louis Dreyfus Company in 2025 as part of Bunge-Viterra clearance — those countries should be considered IN-scope pre-merger Viterra but no longer Bunge/Viterra assets post-merger.

Italy footprint: the brief mentions 'ITA (Lampedusa origin)' but no Viterra-published source for Italy oilseed crushing was confirmed; Viterra Seed LLC mentions a seed-production plant in Italy as part of an Eastern-European seed-development arm, but this is seed breeding rather than commodity origination — Italy intentionally omitted from the commodities list pending stronger evidence.

Viterra's seed-breeding subsidiary (Viterra Seed) operated production sites in Germany, Romania, Hungary and Italy for Eastern European hybrids — these are upstream seed-genetics activities, not grain origination, so they are not modelled as a sourcing footprint.

Smaller country presences mentioned in passing in Viterra corporate materials (37 countries of operation) — including New Zealand, Singapore (trading desk), Switzerland (Glencore parent), UAE, India — were trading offices or logistics nodes rather than asset-backed origination, so not listed as origin/processing countries here.

Pulses (CAN entry) names the US, Australia, Black Sea and Europe as additional Viterra pulse-supply regions in its marketing materials, but per-country processing assets outside Canada/Australia are not separately disclosed.
Viterra.meta_notes: Viterra Limited was acquired by Bunge Global SA in a US$8.2 billion business combination that closed on July 2, 2025. The merged entity is now Bunge Global SA. This file documents Viterra's STANDALONE footprint as it existed pre-merger, so users encountering legacy 'Viterra' references on supply-chain reports can still understand the geography. All claims are as-of dates BEFORE July 2025. For the current state of these assets, consult bunge.json — many Viterra assets are now Bunge assets, but the EU mandated divestiture of Viterra's grains/oilseeds businesses in Hungary and Poland (sold to Louis Dreyfus Company in 2025) as a remedy for clearance. Origins: Saskatchewan Wheat Pool (founded 1924) → reorganized as Viterra Inc. in Canada (2007 merger of Saskatchewan Wheat Pool and Agricore United) → acquired by Glencore plc in 2013 → Glencore folded its agricultural division (Glencore Agri) into Viterra and rebranded the combined global business as 'Viterra' in 2020. Pre-merger Viterra was the largest grain handler in Canada and Australia and a major Black Sea (Ukraine, Russia) trader.

country_claims.evidence: Indonesia is Wilmar's largest palm oil sourcing country. Wilmar discloses that ~66% of its 234,334 ha of oil palm planted area is in Indonesia (Sumatra, West Kalimantan, Central Kalimantan), and Indonesian third-party mills dominate the publicly-published supplier mill list on Wilmar's Sustainability Dashboard.

Wilmar holds ~25% of its planted area in East Malaysia (Sabah/Sarawak) and operates 14 refineries with integrated crushing, seven refineries, one standalone kernel crushing facility and four bulking facilities across Malaysia and Indonesia. Malaysian mills are fully published on Wilmar's supply chain map.

Wilmar entered Ghana through the acquisition of Benso Oil Palm Plantations (BOPP), a company listed on the Ghana Stock Exchange. Wilmar's own mill list includes mills located in Ghana, and Ghana is named in Wilmar's Africa palm portfolio (alongside Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria, Uganda).

Wilmar acquired approximately 30,000 ha of land in Cross River State, Nigeria, for oil palm plantation expansion (PZ Wilmar joint venture). Wilmar publishes a dedicated Nigeria sustainability brief covering its plantation, milling and refining operations in the country.

Côte d'Ivoire is one of four African countries (with Ghana, Nigeria, Uganda) where Wilmar holds oil palm planted area directly or through joint ventures and associates, per Wilmar's plantation business page.

Uganda is named in Wilmar's African palm portfolio (Kalangala / Bidco Uganda partnership). Listed alongside Indonesia, Malaysia, Ghana, Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire in Wilmar's plantation business overview.

Wilmar Sugar Australia (formerly Sucrogen, acquired from CSR in 2010) is Australia's largest raw sugar producer, crushing ~15 million MT of cane annually across 8 mills in Queensland to produce >2 million MT of raw sugar. Wilmar also holds 75% of Sugar Australia which operates refineries at Yarraville (Victoria) and Racecourse (Mackay, Queensland).

Wilmar holds ~62% of Shree Renuka Sugars Limited (SRSL), one of India's largest sugar refiners and ethanol producers. SRSL operates 8 sugar mills in India and two port-based refineries (Kandla on the west coast, Haldia on the east coast).
Through the Sugar Australia / NZ Sugar JV (75% Wilmar), Wilmar operates the Chelsea Sugar refinery in Auckland, New Zealand's only sugar refiner.

Wilmar's Chinese subsidiary Yihai Kerry Arawana (YKA, ~89.99% owned, listed on Shenzhen ChiNext) is one of China's largest oilseed crushers. Wilmar reports total soybean crush in China grew from 86.8 million MT in 2019 to 93.0 million MT in 2020, with continued expansion in oilseed crushing, flour, rice, oleochemicals and consumer products.

AWL Agri Business (formerly Adani Wilmar) is India's largest edible oil refiner and consumer-pack producer (Fortune brand) and a leading oilseed crusher. Wilmar increased its stake to a controlling ~57-66% in 2024-2025 making AWL a Wilmar subsidiary.
Wilmar operates joint-venture soybean crushing capacity in southern Vietnam and has disclosed plans for an additional plant in the north of Vietnam.
Wilmar operates the Yuzhny oil refinery in Ukraine (sunflower oil) — disclosed by Wilmar as the largest refinery in Ukraine. Merged operationally with NMGK in Russia.

Via the NMGK JV, Wilmar discloses crushing plants, oil refineries, margarine, mayonnaise and soap manufacturing in Russia; NMGK is the largest seller of table margarine, mayonnaise and sauces in Russia per Wilmar's own description.

Through Yihai Kerry Arawana, Wilmar is one of China's largest rice millers and flour millers. The Arawana brand has ~45% market share in Chinese consumer-pack edible oils and a substantial position in rice and flour.
AWL Agri Business (Wilmar India) markets rice, wheat flour, pulses, besan and soya value-added products under the Fortune brand in addition to edible oils.
Wilmar lists Vietnam among countries where it holds leading positions in consumer-pack edible oils, rice, flour and related staples.

Wilmar's specialty fats and oleochemicals capacity is concentrated in Indonesia and Malaysia, drawing on its integrated palm oil refining footprint. Disclosed via Wilmar's plantation + processing pages.
Malaysian refineries form a major part of Wilmar's specialty fats / oleochemicals value chain; integrated with the East Malaysian plantation base.
AWL Agri Business (Wilmar India) is described in Wilmar's India brief as the largest branded specialty fats and oleochemicals producer in India.

evidence_gaps: No tonnage by country-of-origin disclosed for any commodity; share_pct fields are NULL by design.
African palm origin volumes (Ghana, Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire, Uganda) are small relative to Indonesia/Malaysia but not split out individually in financial reporting.
Soybeans crushed in China are largely imported (predominantly from Brazil, USA, Argentina) but Wilmar does not publish an origin split for its Chinese crush.
Russia/Ukraine NMGK operations: Wilmar continues to disclose ownership but does not provide an updated 2023-2024 operational status given the war; this is a known transparency gap.
Sucrogen Australia's cane is sourced from third-party growers in Queensland; grower-level origin lists are not as exhaustively published as Wilmar's palm mill list.

Wilmar's Country Statistics traceability page lists more origin countries (e.g. PNG, Thailand, Honduras, Guatemala) that flow into its refineries via third-party traders; these were not included here at country-claim level because they are typically <1% of supply and not explicitly tied to a Wilmar-owned asset.
Wilmar.meta_notes: Wilmar is publicly listed on SGX and is widely cited as having the strongest supply-chain transparency among major palm oil traders. Wilmar publishes a public Sustainability Dashboard, a Supply Chain Map, a Country Statistics page, and downloadable own-mill and supplier mill lists (typically ~1,500+ third-party mills supplying its refineries in Indonesia + Malaysia, plus ~44 own mills across East Malaysia, Indonesia, Ghana and Nigeria). Volumes by country are not consistently disclosed; share-of-volume figures are therefore left NULL. Country claims below are sourced from Wilmar's Annual Report 2024, segment review documents, business pages on wilmar-international.com, and the public traceability dashboard.

Yara International.ownership: public (Oslo Stock Exchange: YAR; OTC ADR: YARIY)

country_claims.evidence: Yara's Porsgrunn facility in Norway is one of Yara's two largest production plants (the other is Sluiskil, Netherlands), operated by the Global Plants & Operational Excellence unit. The site has run an in-house de-N2O catalyst since 2005 reducing CO2-equivalent emissions by ~1.5 million tons annually, and produces ammonia, nitric acid, calcium nitrate (YaraLiva) and NPK compound fertilizers.

Yara Sluiskil is Yara's other flagship production site (alongside Porsgrunn) operated by the Global Plants & Operational Excellence unit; one of Europe's largest ammonia/urea/nitrate complexes. The Sluiskil CCS project is set to capture ~12 million tons of CO2 over 15 years per Yara's own disclosure.

Yara has an Australian subsidiary (Yara Australia) and Yara operates the Yara Pilbara ammonia complex in Western Australia (a wholly-Yara-owned ammonia plant since the 2020 Apache/Norwest divestment to Yara; later partially divested to a Yara-led JV for technical-grade ammonium nitrate). Australia is one of the country subsidiaries listed on Yara's global website.

Yara permanently closed the ammonia plant at Tertre, Belgium in 2022 (urea and CN production at the site was suspended earlier and not restarted as gas-price economics deteriorated). Belgium remains in Yara's commercial network with subsidiary Yara Belgium.

Germany is named by Yara as a Yara Industrial Solutions production-plant country (along with Brazil, South Africa, Sweden, France, Malaysia); Yara has historically operated an ammonia/urea complex at Brunsbüttel and fertilizer/AdBlue plants in Germany. The Rostock fertilizer plant was closed in 2024 per Yara's own news release.

France is named by Yara as a Yara Industrial Solutions production country. Yara operates the Le Havre and Pardies (Mont/Pyrénées) sites in France, producing nitric acid, ammonium nitrate, urea solutions and AdBlue.

Trinidad is one of Yara's offshore (non-European) ammonia supply origins. Yara historically held an equity interest in Trinidad ammonia capacity (via Tringen/Yara Trinidad) feeding its global ammonia trade and shipping network. The Yara Fertilizer Industry Handbook 2025 explicitly lists Trinidad as a top global ammonia export origin in flows to Europe, USA, Latin America, and Morocco.

Yara Sluiskil is Europe's largest integrated nitrogen production site and a major urea producer; Yara identifies Sluiskil as one of its two flagship production plants in the Global Plants & Operational Excellence unit.
Yara Porsgrunn produces urea and urea-based industrial products (including AdBlue feedstock) alongside its ammonia, nitric acid, calcium nitrate and NPK output as Yara's flagship Norwegian site.

Yara Pilbara in Western Australia produces ammonia and (via a JV) technical ammonium nitrate. Australia is one of Yara's principal nitrogen production locations in Asia/Oceania per Yara's regional country listings.

Porsgrunn is Yara's largest single NPK production complex (YaraMila product family); the 'How we make our fertilizers' page identifies Yara's overall NPK + calcium-nitrate output at 13.5 million tons per year (of 26 million tons total fertilizer).

Yara historically operated the Uusikaupunki NPK plant and the Siilinjärvi phosphate mine + integrated fertilizer complex (Yara Suomi). Yara announced and completed the divestment of its Finnish fertilizer business in 2025 — Finland is no longer a Yara production country going forward but remains as a sales/commercial subsidiary listing.
Yara closed the Rostock fertilizer plant (NPK / specialty fertilizers) in 2024 per Yara's own news release citing structural cost pressures on European production.

Yara Brasil is Yara's single largest commercial market and a major production footprint. Brazil is named by Yara as a Yara Industrial Solutions production country, and Yara Brazil operates a large network of NPK blending and granulation plants (acquired largely through Vale Fertilizantes / Bunge Fertilizantes / Galvani transactions including the Cubatão complex).

Yara's Siilinjärvi mine in Finland was the EU's only operating phosphate-rock mine and was an integrated mining + chemical complex producing phosphoric acid and finished NPK fertilizers. Yara completed the divestment of its Finnish business in 2025; Siilinjärvi is therefore no longer part of Yara's phosphate footprint.

Yara Brasil holds phosphate mining and processing assets acquired through the Vale Fertilizantes (2018) and Galvani transactions, with phosphate rock operations supplying NPK and SSP production for the Brazilian market — Yara's largest commercial market.

Brazil is Yara's largest commercial market with a large Yara-branded retail and agronomy network; Yara Brasil is the parent subsidiary listed on Yara's global country switcher and Yara's 10,000+ branded retail outlets are concentrated in Brazil.
Yara North America (yara.us) is one of Yara's regional sales and agronomy units in the Americas region per Yara's global country switcher and Where We Operate page.

Yara India (yara.in) is one of Yara's Africa & Asia sales units serving the world's second-largest fertilizer market; Yara has historically operated urea trading and import operations in India and is one of Yara's largest non-Brazilian commercial geographies.
Yara Argentina (yara.com.ar) is listed in Yara's Americas country subsidiary roster on the global website.
South Africa is named by Yara as both a Yara Industrial Solutions production country and a Yara Africa commercial subsidiary (yara.co.za).
Malaysia is named by Yara as a Yara Industrial Solutions production country (Base Chemicals / Mining Applications / Transport Reagents) and operates as a regional sales hub (yara.my).
Sweden is named by Yara as a Yara Industrial Solutions production country and is listed as a commercial subsidiary (yara.se).
Yara Australia (yara.com.au) is Yara's main Oceania crop-nutrition commercial unit alongside the Pilbara production site.

Yara Kenya (yara.co.ke) is listed among Yara's six African commercial subsidiaries (Ghana, Kenya, Morocco, South Africa, Tanzania, Zambia) — Yara has prioritized smallholder crop-nutrition in sub-Saharan Africa.
Yara Ghana (yara.com.gh) is listed among Yara's six African commercial subsidiaries.
Norway is Yara's HQ country (Drammensveien 131, Oslo) and home to the Porsgrunn flagship plant; Yara Norge (yara.no) is also the domestic commercial unit.

evidence_gaps: No published share-of-volume percentages by country for any commodity. Yara reports financial segments by region (Europe; Americas; Africa & Asia; Global Plants & Operational Excellence; Yara Clean Ammonia; Yara Industrial Solutions) and at the consolidated level (15.7 USD bn revenue, 32.06 million tonnes total deliveries in 2025) — country-level tonnage is not disclosed in primary IR materials.

Plant-by-plant capacity table is not published in standalone form on yara.com; the Fertilizer Industry Handbook 2025 (Yara IR) contains global capacity/trade-flow charts but does not enumerate Yara's own plant capacities country-by-country. This JSON relies on Yara's 'Our organization' narrative and individual plant news releases.

Russia exposure: Yara has no production in Russia and the Acron / Belevet partnership history is NOT confirmed via Yara's own IR materials in this research session. Yara historically held a non-controlling stake in OAO Acron-related Russian assets through legacy Norsk Hydro positions but I could not find an active Yara-controlled URL describing current Russia exposure — flagged as evidence gap. The 'Where we operate' country switcher does NOT list Russia.

Libya (LIFECO joint venture with the Libyan National Oil Corp and GECOL) is widely understood to be a Yara ammonia/urea JV but I could not confirm via an active yara.com URL in this research session — excluded from the country list rather than guessed at.

USA (Belle Plaine, Saskatchewan-adjacent ammonia or US-Gulf greenfield projects with BHP): Yara has announced multiple low-carbon ammonia partnership studies but no operating US wholly-owned ammonia plant is confirmed via current Yara material — USA in this JSON appears only as a commercial unit (Yara North America) not as a production country.

Estonia (Yara Suomi distribution), Hungary (historical Hódmezővásárhely NPK), and Italy (historical Ferrara) — these European production sites do not appear in the current 'Our organization' narrative; status is unclear (likely closed or divested) — excluded from the country list pending Yara source confirmation.

Post-2022 European nitrogen plant closures CONFIRMED in this research: (1) Tertre, Belgium ammonia plant — permanently closed 2022; (2) Rostock, Germany fertilizer plant — closed 2024; (3) Finland fertilizer business (incl. Siilinjärvi and Uusikaupunki) — divested in full in 2025. Additional European curtailments (Yara cut up to 40% of European ammonia output in autumn 2022 due to gas prices) are referenced in Yara press releases but were largely restored to operation rather than permanently closed.

Of the ~37 sales-subsidiary countries visible on Yara's country switcher (Europe 24 + Americas 12 + Asia-Oceania 10 + Africa 6 = 52 entries, with several language-variants), only the largest 11 commercial markets are listed here — adding the remaining smaller commercial-only subsidiaries (e.g. Bolivia, Costa Rica, Czech Republic, Greece, Bulgaria, Croatia, Latvia, Lithuania, Serbia, New Zealand, Philippines, Thailand, Kazakhstan, Vietnam, Indonesia, Korea, China, Tanzania, Zambia, Morocco, etc.) would expand the commercial footprint but with no incremental production signal.
Yara International.meta_notes: Yara is the world's largest nitrogen fertilizer producer outside China and the industry's only truly global crop nutrition company. Yara reports operations in more than 60 countries with sales in around 140 countries and approximately 26 production plants (per the 'Where we operate' page). Yara's business is fertilizer PRODUCTION (heavy nitrogen/ammonia footprint in Europe, plus large NPK and phosphate footprint in Brazil and Finland) and COMMERCIAL/RETAIL distribution (large Yara-branded retail network in Brazil, plus regional sales companies in ~140 countries). Yara reports financial segments by region (Europe; Americas; Africa & Asia; Global Plants & Operational Excellence; Yara Clean Ammonia; Yara Industrial Solutions) rather than by country tonnage — country-level production tonnages and country-level commercial volumes are not disclosed in primary IR materials. Yara has no production in Russia, but historically had a non-controlling stake in Belarusian potash through OAO Belaruskali / has long since divested — see evidence_gaps. Yara confirmed plant closures and divestments in Europe post-2022 (Tertre, Belgium ammonia plant permanently closed 2022; Rostock, Germany fertilizer plant closed 2024; Finnish fertilizer business divested 2025) reflecting the European nitrogen-cost squeeze.
